title: Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act
summary: This page from the U.S. Treasury provides an informational overview of FATCA (Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act), enacted in 2010, and lists the intergovernmental agreements (IGAs) between the U.S. and foreign jurisdictions for FATCA implementation. The content is a static reference page cataloging which countries have IGAs in force, signed, or in substance.
key_points: FATCA was enacted in 2010 by Congress to target non-compliance by U.S. taxpayers using foreign accounts
Foreign financial institutions (FFIs) must report to the IRS information about financial accounts held by U.S. taxpayers or foreign entities with U.S. taxpayer ownership
FFIs comply either by registering directly with the IRS or through Intergovernmental Agreements (IGAs) with their jurisdictions
The page provides a table of jurisdictions with their IGA status: In Force, Signed, Agreement in Substance, or Superseded
Most IGAs were treated as in effect starting June 30, 2014 or November 30, 2014
Two IGA model types exist: Model 1 (reporting to local tax authority) and Model 2 (direct reporting to IRS)
why_it_matters: This source is not relevant to market structure or macro catalysts. FATCA is a tax compliance mechanism enacted in 2010—regulatory reference material that does not provide market-moving information or trading signals.

issues_found: Source is a static informational reference page cataloging FATCA IGA status by jurisdiction, not market-moving content
Topic/macro catalysts classification is incorrect - FATCA is tax compliance infrastructure, not a macro catalyst or market-moving event
No published_at date provided in source metadata, source appears to be evergreen reference material
Content is primarily a jurisdictional listing/table rather than substantive research or analysis
Source has minimal relevance to trading decisions or market structure as defined
This is administrative/reference material that does not provide actionable market information
